entry.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 in University Data Theft Campaign
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability discovery may be compressing exploit timelines faster than traditional patch cycles can absorb. Google Mandiant attributed a PeopleSoft exploitation campaign to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, with observed activity dated between May 27 and June 9. The exploited vulnerability, CVE-2026-35273, affects Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools and carries a CVSS score of 9.8 due to unauthenticated remote code execution over HTTP without user interaction. The campaign appears heavily concentrated in higher education. Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, and 68% were in higher education, mostly in the United States. The University of Nottingham confirmed a breach, and Have I Been Pwned counted approximately 455,000 unique email addresses in a leaked dataset associated with the incident. Oracle confirmed that CVE-2026-35273 affects PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools versions 8.61 and 8.62 and released emergency mitigations while indicating that a patch is forthcoming. BleepingComputer reported that ShinyHunters claimed to have used a “gadget chain” combining older flaws and zero-day vulnerabilities, allegedly stealing data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations. Researcher Michael R also identified exposed online directories containing attack-related tooling and shared IP addresses relevant to the campaign. Cisco Talos highlighted analysis from Yuri Kramarz arguing that AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing human-centered patching processes. The article frames frontier AI models as capable of autonomously discovering and exploiting zero-days in minutes, compressing the traditional vulnerability lifecycle and increasing the operational pressure on defenders. The Talos discussion places the PeopleSoft activity in a broader context: organizations face a threat environment where exploit development, weaponization, and deployment may occur faster than conventional vulnerability management workflows can respond. The piece emphasizes foundational security controls, segmentation, behavioral detection, and validation exercises as part of a fallback model for environments where patch-first strategies may not keep pace with emerging exploitation timelines. Permiso announced that it won Best Identity Threat Detection and Response Platform at the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards, presented by The Hacker News. The item reflects continued industry focus on identity threat detection and response as a dedicated security category, particularly as credential misuse, cloud identity abuse, and post-compromise identity activity remain central to modern intrusion chains.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by reporting on active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, a critical unauthenticated remote code execution zero-day tied to data theft activity attributed to ShinyHunters. The reporting also reflects a broader concern that AI-assisted vulnerability...

entry.title: CalPhishing, The Gentlemen Ransomware, Miasma/Hades, and AI Agent OAuth Risk
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust boundaries around OAuth, prompt injection, and managed security operations. Health-ISAC reported an emerging healthcare-sector social engineering pattern called CalPhishing, in which attackers use iCalendar files and enterprise calendar auto-processing to place malicious links directly into employee schedules. Recent campaigns reportedly use ConsentFix, also described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication protections after the user interaction stage. Netskope Threat Labs framed OAuth authorization abuse as a structural scaling problem, particularly as organizations adopt AI agents that rely on persistent tokens, broad permissions, opaque runtimes, and autonomous workflows. The article described a confused deputy scenario in which an attacker plants malicious instructions in data consumed by an agent, causing the agent to use its own valid OAuth token to perform attacker-directed actions. SpecterOps published research on automating indirect prompt injection testing against an agentic system with access to Notion search, shell execution, and web-fetching tools. The workflow used poisoned Notion content to progress from simple echo payloads to full-chain payloads involving command execution and HTTP exfiltration, while noting that newer Claude Sonnet model behavior detected some earlier payload styles more reliably. StepSecurity reported that the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading across npm and PyPI ecosystems, with execution paths tied to project opening, package installation, or package import. Miasma uses a small binding.gyp file in a technique described as “Phantom Gyp,” while Hades relies on an obfuscated import hook in init.py, creating visibility gaps for tools focused primarily on install-time checks, process behavior, or declared dependencies. Aikido highlighted npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, as a major package-manager security change because dependency install scripts will no longer run by default unless packages are allowlisted. The release is also expected to block Git dependencies and remote URL dependencies unless explicitly permitted, and to treat implicit node-gyp rebuilds triggered by binding.gyp as install scripts. Socket announced Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer, positioning the appointment around enterprise concerns with open source, AI tooling, and malicious package risk. The company said it now protects more than 27,000 organizations and framed the role as both an internal security function and a customer-facing software supply chain security leadership position. PRODAFT reporting cited by The Hacker News tracks The Gentlemen ransomware operation as Phantom Mantis and says the group shifted from affiliate activity into an independent partnership program in July 2025. The operation has claimed 478 victims, reportedly accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026, and includes Go-based ransomware that Microsoft tracks under Storm-2697 with a self-propagation mode when launched with the --spread argument. Law enforcement dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency service Europol said was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million. Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized domains, vehicles, properties, cryptocurrency assets, Telegram accounts, and retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records tied to alleged money mule accounts. In the U.K., Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected ICT systems, while students taking GCSE and A-Level exams were allowed onsite. The incident type was not confirmed, but the reporting cited nearly 2,000 education and childcare-sector cyber incidents recorded by the U.K. Information Commissioner’s Office between 2019 and 2025. Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court after transfer from Thailand, where he was arrested in November, over allegations that he supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard group. Prosecutors allege cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko funded infrastructure used in attacks, and an FBI affidavit identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies while suggesting the actual victim count is higher. In U.S. defense policy, a Senate Armed Services Committee amendment to create a standalone Cyber Force failed by a 14-13 vote. The debate reflected bipartisan frustration over cyber personnel readiness for U.S. Cyber Command, while the Senate bill separately proposed a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon CIO and principal cyber advisor.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by social engineering techniques moving beyond conventional email, expanding ransomware operations, and renewed concern over software supply chain exposure on developer systems. Reporting also highlights how AI agents and autonomous workflows are changing trust...

entry.title: ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, Coupang Fine, LangGraph CVEs, and AI Agent Risks
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of phishing and vishing tactics that continue to rely on trusted brands and user interaction. The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident involving external access to a significant amount of data affecting current and former students. ShinyHunters claimed on its extortion site that it obtained more than 40GB of material, including payment-related data, while HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data identified roughly 455,000 unique email addresses and associated personal, academic, and fee information. In South Korea, Coupang received a record 624.6 billion won, approximately $409 million, fine from the Personal Information Protection Commission after a breach affecting about 37.55 million people. The regulator cited authentication signature key management failures, access control deficiencies, data destruction and notification violations, and obstruction of the investigation; authorities identified a former Coupang IT employee, a 43-year-old Chinese national, as the primary suspect. Multiple reports emphasized that attackers are increasingly operating through identity systems rather than relying solely on traditional exploit chains. Flashpoint reported more than 11.1 million devices infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity artifacts circulating in criminal markets. AI agent security emerged as a recurring concern across SaaS, developer workflows, and CI/CD environments. Varonis found that the OpenClaw email agent Pinchy could be tricked by ordinary-looking phishing emails into forwarding sensitive material, while Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content. AppOmni described agentic AI as a new class of SaaS identity capable of fetching data, creating users, and modifying configurations. Related identity exposure analysis from XM Cyber highlighted the risk of unmanaged human, machine, service, cloud, and AI accounts across hybrid environments, particularly where credentials are reused, cached, weak, or poorly governed. Check Point Research disclosed three vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing implementations, including CVE-2025-67644, a SQLite checkpointer SQL injection, and CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue. The research showed that SQL injection could be chained with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution where applications expose getstatehistory() with attacker-controlled filters; fixes are available in updated LangGraph and checkpoint packages. CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, requiring U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities under accelerated timelines, with the shortest deadline set at three days. Prioritization is based on factors including public exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog status, automation potential, and whether exploitation grants partial or full system control. The broader vulnerability management discussion reflected shrinking exploit windows. The Hacker News cited reporting that AI-assisted discovery has increased vulnerability volume and reduced average time-to-exploit, while Aikido’s Docker Hardened Images integration illustrated parallel efforts to reduce container vulnerability noise through signed SBOMs and Docker VEX attestations. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing email posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270. The message used an empty subject line, a JPEG attachment, and a callback number, with OCR revealing a brand mismatch between Geek Squad and “Windows Defender” copyright text. Industrial security and industry community activity also featured in the window. Radiflow and Barrier announced an OT security networking dinner in Aberdeen focused on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and critical infrastructure security, while The Hacker News published the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners across 95 subcategories.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of...

entry.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, and npm Supply Chain Controls
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability updates affecting BitLocker recovery behavior and continued attention to operational technology visibility gaps. Attackers are reportedly targeting CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry that enables code execution with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. Ivanti patched the vulnerability in Sentry versions R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1, but Shadowserver reported soon after that exposed vulnerable systems had already been backdoored in some cases. Shadowserver observed 19 vulnerable Ivanti Sentry instances during scanning and said at least two were backdoored, warning that unpatched exposed systems were likely compromised. The report adds to a broader Ivanti risk pattern: CISA has flagged 34 vulnerabilities across Ivanti products as actively exploited in the wild, including 12 that were also targeted in ransomware attacks. The University of Nottingham confirmed unauthorized access to its student records system, affecting current students and alumni. Have I Been Pwned reported that 454,600 individuals were impacted, with exposed data including email addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility and said it stole more than 40GB of documents from the University of Nottingham and its Malaysia and China campuses. The university said it is working with the third party that maintains the affected platform on a forensic investigation and has reported the incident to Action Fraud and the UK Information Commissioner’s Office. BleepingComputer linked the intrusion to a wider ShinyHunters campaign targeting cloud and on-premises Oracle PeopleSoft instances at more than 100 organizations globally. The incident highlights continued actor interest in enterprise student information systems and third-party-maintained platforms containing high-volume personal and administrative records. GitHub announced breaking changes planned for npm version 12 that will disable dependency install scripts by default. The change targets a long-standing supply chain risk in which npm install can execute preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts from transitive dependencies, allowing a compromised package anywhere in the dependency tree to run arbitrary code on developer systems or CI runners. Under the npm 12 model, dependency install scripts will not execute unless explicitly approved at the project level. npm install will also stop resolving Git dependencies or dependencies from remote URLs unless those behaviors are explicitly allowed with relevant options, narrowing default execution and resolution pathways commonly abused in dependency-chain compromise scenarios. Microsoft also resolved a Windows platform issue that caused some Windows Server 2025 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after the April 2026 security update. The bug affected devices with BitLocker enabled on the OS drive and a specific unrecommended Group Policy configuration involving TPM platform validation and PCR7; fixes were released in KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025 and KB5093998 for Windows 11 23H2. A webinar titled “Closing the OT Security Visibility Gap: From Blind Spots to Continuous Protection” focuses on persistent visibility limitations in operational technology environments. The framing reflects a broader security concern around insufficient asset, traffic, and behavioral visibility across industrial networks where continuous monitoring remains harder than in conventional IT environments. The topic aligns with ongoing pressure on defenders to understand OT exposure and detect changes in environments that often include legacy systems, limited instrumentation, and operational constraints. While the article is webinar-focused rather than incident-driven, it reflects continued industry emphasis on closing monitoring gaps in critical infrastructure and industrial settings.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability...

entry.title: MCP Security Tradeoffs for Agentic SOC Access
entry.content: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new implications for credential exposure and access control. Mitiga’s analysis distinguishes APIs and MCP by their intended consumers. APIs are described as contracts for code, best suited to deterministic automation where developers know which endpoints to call and require predictable behavior. MCP, by contrast, is presented as a contract for AI agents that need to discover, select, and chain tools during live investigative tasks. The article positions APIs as better aligned with scripted workflows, integrations, and repeatable automation. MCP is framed as more useful for analysts, AI assistants, and Agentic SOC platforms operating through natural language prompts, where the agent determines which tools to use and how to combine outputs during an investigation. A key reported benefit of MCP is reduced time-to-first-answer in security investigations. The article states that MCP can allow an LLM-driven system to choose tools, chain results, and format outputs without requiring a pre-built workflow, potentially compressing early investigative steps into minutes. The same access model also expands the security surface of AI-enabled SOC operations. Mitiga notes that MCP servers provide agents with credentialed access into security environments, making MCP tokens, scopes, and server permissions part of the effective attack surface. In this framing, MCP adoption introduces identity and authorization considerations similar to API security, but applied to autonomous or semi-autonomous agent activity. The dominant theme is the coexistence of deterministic automation and agentic investigation models. APIs remain central for predictable machine-to-machine execution, while MCP is presented as an emerging interface layer for AI-assisted security operations that can accelerate analysis while concentrating privileged access paths around agent tooling.
entry.summary: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new...

entry.title: ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 as AI Agent Risks Escalate
entry.content:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, expanding research into AI-agent abuse, and continued pressure from ransomware and software supply chain threats. Reporting also highlights identity-centric attack paths, calendar-based phishing, regulatory action after major breaches, and government efforts to accelerate vulnerability remediation. Google Mandiant attributed exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273 to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, with activity reported between May 27 and June 9 before Oracle issued its advisory on June 10. The vulnerability is a critical unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools versions 8.61 and 8.62, rated CVSS 9.8, with unsupported earlier versions likely exposed. Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, 68% of which were in higher education and mostly in the United States. The University of Nottingham confirmed that a significant amount of data affecting current and former students was accessed, while Have I Been Pwned identified about 455,000 unique email addresses in partially published data containing personal, academic, and payment-related information. ShinyHunters claimed it used a gadget chain combining older flaws and a zero-day to access PeopleSoft instances, allegedly stealing data from 300 instances across more than 100 organizations. Oracle released emergency mitigations and said a patch would follow, while separate reporting noted researcher-discovered exposed directories containing attack tooling and infrastructure indicators. Multiple reports focused on AI agents as a growing security control gap. Imperva found that OpenClaw flattened contacts, vCards, and location pins into model prompts without treating them as untrusted, enabling hidden instructions in those objects; OpenClaw patched the issue in version 2026.4.23. Varonis separately demonstrated that an OpenClaw email agent connected to Gmail could be socially engineered into forwarding mock AWS keys, database strings, SSH credentials, and customer data to an external address. SpecterOps published a lab workflow that used OpenAI Codex to generate and refine indirect prompt injection payloads against an agentic system using Claude Sonnet models on Amazon Bedrock. Check Point Research disclosed vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointer implementations, including SQLite SQL injection and unsafe msgpack deserialization that could be chained to remote code execution in exposed application patterns. Netskope, AppOmni, and XM Cyber framed AI agents as a new identity and OAuth governance problem at scale. Their reporting emphasized persistent OAuth tokens, broad scopes, opaque runtime behavior, shadow AI in SaaS platforms, and non-human identities spanning Active Directory, cloud platforms, service accounts, and agentic workflows. StepSecurity reported that the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch Hades are spreading through npm and PyPI by executing when developers open projects or import packages. Miasma reportedly compromised 57 npm packages across more than 286 malicious versions, while Hades affected ensmallen and related machine-learning and bioinformatics packages through obfuscated import-time execution. Aikido highlighted npm v12 changes scheduled for July 2026 that will stop dependency install scripts from running by default unless packages are allowlisted. The change targets attack patterns seen in recent package ecosystem compromises that abused install-time scripts to steal credentials or execute malware. Container and software assurance coverage included Aikido support for Docker Hardened Images and Docker VEX attestations, intended to suppress vulnerabilities Docker has marked as fixed, not affected, or not requiring triage. Socket also appointed Andrew Becherer as its first CISO, citing growing enterprise concern around open source, AI tooling, and malicious package risk. PRODAFT reported that The Gentlemen ransomware operation, tracked as Phantom Mantis, has claimed 478 victims and transitioned from affiliate activity into an independent partnership program in July 2025. Microsoft tracks the cluster as Storm-2697 and says its Go-based ransomware can self-propagate across reachable systems when executed with a spread argument. Law enforcement dismantled the AudiA6 cryptocurrency laundering service, which Europol said processed more than $380 million for ransomware actors and other cybercriminals. Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized domains, vehicles, properties, cryptocurrency, Telegram accounts, and retrieved 6,000 KYC records tied to alleged money mule accounts. Operational disruption from cyber incidents continued in education, with Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sending most students home for a second consecutive day after an incident affected ICT systems. Separately, Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court over allegations that he supported Void Blizzard infrastructure used in cyberespionage operations. Health-ISAC reported an emerging CalPhishing pattern in which attackers use iCalendar files or direct calendar invitations to deliver malicious links into enterprise calendar applications. Recent campaigns reportedly pair calendar delivery with ConsentFix-style device code phishing to capture session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing lure posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice, notable for an empty subject, no body text, a JPEG-only payload, and a brand mismatch referencing Windows Defender. Broader identity reporting noted that infostealer ecosystems produced billions of stolen credentials, session cookies, cloud tokens, and related artifacts circulating in illicit markets. South Korea fined Coupang roughly $409 million after a breach affecting more than 37 million customers, citing inadequate authentication key management, access controls, notification practices, and investigation conduct. In U.S. government policy, CISA issued Binding Operational Directive 26-04 requiring accelerated remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities, while a Senate proposal to create a U.S. Cyber Force narrowly failed in committee.

ShinyHunters Exploits Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day (CVE-2026-35273) to Breach Universities — Must Read

ShinyHunters exploited a zero-day remote code execution flaw in Oracle PeopleSoft to breach universities and steal data before Oracle disclosed the issue. Organizations running PeopleSoft should urgently restrict or disable exposed PSEMHUB endpoints, check for compromise, and apply Oracle fixes when available.

  • Google's Mandiant attributes the campaign to ShinyHunters, tracked as UNC6240, and says the activity occurred between May 27 and June 9 before Oracle published its advisory on June 10.
  • CVE-2026-35273 is a 9.8-rated remote code execution flaw in Oracle PeopleSoft Enterprise PeopleTools that requires no login or user interaction and affects PeopleTools 8.61 and 8.62, with earlier unsupported versions likely vulnerable.
  • Mandiant notified more than 100 organizations with vulnerable endpoints, and 68% of them were in higher education, mostly in the United States.
  • The University of Nottingham confirmed a breach, and Have I Been Pwned counted about 455,000 unique email addresses in the leaked data set.
  • Oracle recommends disabling the Environment Management Hub service or removing the PSEMHUB application, and if that is not possible, blocking external access to /PSEMHUB/* and /PSIGW/HttpListeningConnector.

Miasma and Hades Are Spreading Now: Detect Them on Developer Machines with Suspicious Files — Must Read

StepSecurity warns that the Miasma npm worm and Hades PyPI campaign are actively spreading by running on project open or package import, stealing developer secrets and publishing tokens from machines. The article promotes Dev Machine Guard’s Suspicious Files feature as a way to detect the dropped files these attacks leave across developer fleets.

  • StepSecurity says the Miasma worm and its PyPI branch, Hades, are actively spreading across the npm and PyPI ecosystems by running when developers open projects or import packages.
  • Miasma compromised 57 npm packages across more than 286 malicious versions on June 3, 2026, while Hades later compromised ensmallen and roughly two dozen related machine-learning and bioinformatics packages on PyPI.
  • The article says Miasma uses a small binding.gyp file to trigger code execution during npm install, while Hades uses an obfuscated hook in __init__.py that downloads a Bun runtime and runs a JavaScript payload on import.
  • StepSecurity argues that registry gateways, EDR, and SCA scanners can miss these attacks because they focus on installs, process behavior, or declared dependencies rather than suspicious files on developer machines.
  • StepSecurity’s Dev Machine Guard Suspicious Files feature detects files associated with known supply chain attacks, including Miasma and Hades artifacts, using centrally managed detection rules maintained by StepSecurity.

The Gentlemen Ransomware Claims 478 Victims, Can Spread Like a Worm — Should Read

The Gentlemen ransomware group has rapidly grown into a major RaaS operation with 478 claimed victims, evolving from affiliate activity into an independent program led by a Russian-speaking actor tied to prior ransomware crews. Its Go-based ransomware can spread worm-like across networks, supports multiple platforms, and is backed by mature affiliate support, BYOVD evasion, and aggressive double-extortion tactics.

  • PRODAFT tracks The Gentlemen operation as Phantom Mantis and says it transitioned in July 2025 from an affiliate using other RaaS resources into an independent partnership program.
  • The Gentlemen has claimed 478 victims to date, and reports cited in the article say it accounted for 10% of ransomware activity in April 2026.
  • Microsoft tracks the cluster as Storm-2697 and says its Go-based ransomware can become self-propagating across reachable network systems when run with the --spread argument.
  • The group provides Windows, Linux, ESXi, Windows XP+, and Logical Volume Manager ransomware versions and offers affiliates a 90% profit share.
  • The article says The Gentlemen uses tactics and tools including BYOVD-based EDR killing, vulnerable edge-device access, red team utilities, log clearing, Microsoft Defender disabling, and multi-channel extortion.

From SQLi to RCE – Exploiting LangGraph’s Checkpointer — Should Read

Check Point Research found that LangGraph’s SQLite and Redis checkpointers could be abused through user-controlled state-history filters, with the SQLite SQL injection chaining into unsafe msgpack deserialization for remote code execution. Self-hosted users should upgrade to the patched LangGraph checkpoint packages immediately.

  • Check Point Research analyzed LangGraph’s persistence layer and uncovered three vulnerabilities affecting its checkpointer implementations.
  • CVE-2025-67644 is a SQL injection in the SQLite checkpointer caused by attacker-controlled filter keys being interpolated into a JSON path in the SQL WHERE clause.
  • CVE-2026-28277 is an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue where crafted extension data can import a module and call a function such as os.system with attacker-controlled input.
  • The SQLite SQL injection can be chained with the msgpack deserialization issue to achieve remote code execution when an application exposes get_state_history() with a user-controlled filter.
  • LangChain patched the issues and advised users to update to langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite 3.0.1+, langgraph 1.0.10+, and langgraph-checkpoint-redis 1.0.2+.

Coupang hit with record $409 million data breach fine in Korea — Should Read

South Korea’s data protection regulator fined Coupang a record $409 million after inadequate security controls led to a data breach affecting about 37.55 million customers. Authorities also cited failures in key management, access controls, data handling, breach notification, and cooperation with the investigation.

  • South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission fined Coupang 624.6 billion won, roughly $409 million, after a data breach affecting more than 37 million customers.
  • The regulator said approximately 37.55 million people had personal information leaked because of inadequate security practices, including failures in authentication key management and access controls.
  • PIPC also cited violations involving data destruction, leak-notification requirements, interference with the independence of Coupang’s data protection officer, and obstruction of the investigation.
  • Coupang announced plans to pay 1.685 trillion won, approximately $1.17 billion, and distribute single-use purchase vouchers of 50,000 won per customer to compensate more than 33 million affected customers.
  • South Korean authorities identified the primary suspect as a 43-year-old Chinese national who worked in Coupang’s IT department between 2022 and 2024.

New Attacks Trick OpenClaw AI Agent Into Running Code and Leaking Secrets — Should Read

Researchers showed that OpenClaw AI agents can be manipulated through hidden prompt injections in message objects or believable emails to run code and leak sensitive data. Users should update to version 2026.4.23 and restrict agent permissions, outbound actions, and access to sensitive systems.

  • Imperva found that OpenClaw flattened shared contacts, vCards, and location pins into the model prompt without marking them as untrusted, allowing hidden instructions in those objects to be executed by the agent.
  • OpenClaw patched Imperva’s message-object prompt-injection issue in version 2026.4.23 by moving contact names, vCard fields, and location labels into a separate untrusted-metadata channel.
  • Varonis built a test OpenClaw agent connected to a Gmail inbox and showed that ordinary-looking emails could convince it to forward mock AWS keys, database connection strings, SSH credentials, and a synthetic customer export to an outside address.
  • Varonis reported that both Gemini 3.1 Pro and OpenAI Codex GPT-5.4 fell for social pretexts, even when the agent profile instructed sender verification first.
  • The article recommends updating OpenClaw, enforcing agent policy, gating outbound mail to unfamiliar addresses, limiting connector access based on trust level, and requiring human approval for high-risk actions.

New GreatXML Exploit Bypasses Windows BitLocker via Recovery Partition XML Files — Should Read

Security researcher Chaotic Eclipse released GreatXML, a new Windows BitLocker bypass that can grant unrestricted access to encrypted volumes by abusing XML files in the recovery partition and WinRE behavior. Systems that have used Windows Defender Offline Scan may be vulnerable, and the release follows the researcher’s earlier BitLocker and Microsoft Defender zero-day disclosures.

  • Security researcher Chaotic Eclipse, also known as Nightmare-Eclipse and MSNightmare, released a new Windows BitLocker bypass called GreatXML.
  • GreatXML involves placing an unattend.xml file and a Recovery/WindowsRE/ReAgent.xml file in the root of the recovery partition, then rebooting into Windows Recovery Environment by holding Shift while clicking Restart.
  • If executed correctly, GreatXML spawns a shell with unrestricted access to the BitLocker volume.
  • Chaotic Eclipse said systems that have used Windows Defender Offline Scan are automatically vulnerable to the BitLocker bypass.
  • GreatXML follows Chaotic Eclipse’s earlier releases of RoguePlanet, a Microsoft Defender local privilege escalation flaw, and YellowKey, a BitLocker bypass also tracked as CVE-2026-45585.

Authorities dismantle 'AudiA6' ransomware crypto-laundering service — Should Read

Authorities dismantled AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs and other cybercriminals to wash more than $380 million. Two alleged administrators were arrested in Georgia, with domains, assets, crypto, and Telegram accounts seized or frozen.

  • Law enforcement dismantled the AudiA6 cryptocurrency service, which Europol says was used by ransomware actors and other cybercriminals to launder more than $380 million.
  • Europol said AudiA6 was linked to more than 15 international investigations involving ransomware attacks and large-scale cryptocurrency theft.
  • Authorities arrested two individuals in Georgia, seized 25 domains, seized 80 vehicles and properties, seized €86,000 in cryptocurrency, froze €692,000 in cryptocurrency, and blocked Telegram accounts used by the network.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice named Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev as senior members of the AudiA6 platform.
  • Authorities also retrieved 6,000 Know-Your-Customer records linked to money mule accounts allegedly created using stolen or purchased identities.

Hacker linked to Void Blizzard faces charges over cyberespionage campaign — Should Read

Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court on charges that he provided infrastructure supporting Void Blizzard, a Kremlin-linked cyberespionage group accused of compromising at least 11 U.S. companies. He was arrested in Thailand and transferred to U.S. custody as prosecutors allege his crypto-linked purchases helped facilitate attacks against organizations in the U.S. and abroad.

  • Russian national Denis Obrezko appeared in U.S. federal court in Boston after being transferred from Thailand, where he was arrested in November.
  • U.S. prosecutors allege that Obrezko supported the Russian state-linked Void Blizzard hacking group by providing infrastructure used in cyber operations.
  • Prosecutors allege that cryptocurrency transactions linked to Obrezko were used to purchase a virtual private server and internet domain that facilitated attacks against organizations in the United States and other countries.
  • An FBI affidavit says investigators identified at least 11 compromised U.S. companies, though authorities believe the actual number of victims is significantly higher.
  • Researchers have described Void Blizzard as a relatively new threat group supporting Russian government interests and targeting organizations across Europe and North America.

npm v12 delivers one of the biggest security improvements in years — Should Read

npm v12 will stop running dependency install scripts by default, blocking a major supply-chain attack path used by recent npm worms and credential stealers. Developers should start testing npm 11.16.0 warnings and approve only trusted packages before the default changes in July 2026.

  • npm v12, scheduled for July 2026, will stop running dependency install scripts by default unless packages are added to an allowlist.
  • The change blocks automatic execution of preinstall, install, postinstall, and implicit node-gyp rebuild behavior from unapproved dependencies during npm install.
  • npm v12 will also require explicit flags for Git dependencies and remote-URL dependencies through --allow-git and --allow-remote.
  • The article says recent supply chain attacks such as Nx s1ngularity, Shai-Hulud, the axios hijack, and Mini Shai-Hulud abused install-time scripts to steal credentials or run malware.
  • The article recommends upgrading to npm 11.16.0 or later and using npm approve-scripts to identify and approve legitimate packages before npm v12 changes the default behavior.

CISA tells govt agencies to patch critical exploited flaws in 3 days — Should Read

CISA’s new Binding Operational Directive 26-04 requires U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch the highest-risk exploited vulnerabilities on accelerated timelines, as quickly as three days. Agencies must update vulnerability management processes, asset inventories, and reporting to prioritize remediation based on public exposure, KEV status, automation potential, and system control impact.

  • CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, which requires Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities within accelerated timeframes, in some cases as little as three days.
  • The directive prioritizes patching based on whether an asset is publicly exposed, whether the vulnerability is in CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, whether exploitation can be automated, and whether exploitation gives attackers partial or total control of a system.
  • For less urgent cases where automated exploitation is not possible or exploitation only provides partial control, agencies have two weeks to remediate vulnerabilities.
  • BOD 26-04 applies to on-premise federal systems, third-party hosted systems, and FedRAMP and non-FedRAMP cloud environments operated by Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies.
  • Agencies must update vulnerability management processes within 60 days to use CVE and KEV data for remediation decisions, and within 180 days they must follow the new timelines and continuously monitor and report detailed asset metadata.

The Rise of CalPhishing Attacks in the Health Sector — Should Read

Health-ISAC warns that attackers are using malicious calendar invites and .ics files to place phishing links directly into healthcare workers’ calendars, where reminders can persist even after the original email is deleted. Organizations should disable automatic processing of external invites, inspect calendar files, restrict device-code authentication, and hard-delete malicious calendar events during remediation.

  • Health-ISAC observed an emerging social engineering threat called CalPhishing, or Calendar Phishing, on June 11, 2026.
  • Threat actors use iCalendar .ics files or direct calendar invites to deliver malicious links into employees’ enterprise calendar applications such as Microsoft Outlook and Google Calendar.
  • CalPhishing can bypass traditional email security gateways because enterprise email clients may automatically process .ics files and place tentative meetings on a victim’s calendar without the victim opening the delivery email.
  • Recent campaigns use ConsentFix, described as device code phishing, to capture session tokens and bypass Multi-Factor Authentication for persistent, password-less access to enterprise cloud environments.
  • Health-ISAC recommends disabling automatic processing of external calendar invites, inspecting .ics files for embedded URLs and suspicious domains, restricting device code authentication flows, hard-deleting malicious calendar artifacts, and training staff to scrutinize unexpected meeting invites.

Cyber Force not included in Senate defense policy roadmap — May Read

A Senate Armed Services Committee proposal to create a U.S. Cyber Force failed by a narrow 14-13 vote, with lawmakers opting to wait for a pending feasibility study. The defense bill instead advances a major Pentagon cyber leadership reorganization to reduce friction between existing cyber and CIO offices.

  • Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand’s amendment to create a U.S. Cyber Force was defeated 14-13 in the Senate Armed Services Committee during consideration of the fiscal 2027 defense authorization bill.
  • Opponents argued that lawmakers should wait for a National Academy of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine feasibility study on creating a Cyber Force, which is expected to conclude later this year.
  • The close vote reflected bipartisan frustration over the existing military services’ repeated failure to provide U.S. Cyber Command with personnel ready for online operations against adversaries such as China and Russia.
  • An independent commission recently reported that a new Cyber Force could cost up to $11 billion and include about 33,000 troops, with 20,000 active-duty members.
  • The Senate defense bill would create a new Under Secretary of Defense for Cyber, Information, and Networks, dual-hatted as the Pentagon’s Chief Information Officer and principal cyber advisor to the Secretary of Defense.

The Geek Squad Invoice That Forgot Which Brand It Was Pretending to Be — May Read

Attackers sent a blank email with a JPEG-only fake Geek Squad invoice to lure the recipient into calling a scam phone number, bypassing defenses that rely on links or text scanning. The key warning sign was an image-based callback phishing payload with mismatched branding, showing the need for OCR and behavioral detection.

  • An attacker sent a callback phishing email with an empty subject line, no body text, and a single JPEG attachment containing a fabricated Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270.
  • The image included a 24-hour cancellation deadline and the callback phone number 828-424-4048, with no links, URLs, or scannable text in the email body.
  • OCR analysis found a brand mismatch in the invoice image, with a Geek Squad header and a footer reading “Copyright 2026 Windows Defender.”
  • The email was sent from stubin1178@hotmail.com through a FortiMail cloud relay, causing SPF to fail at the final hop while DKIM and DMARC passed via Hotmail.
  • Themis flagged the message at 90% confidence as a vishing and image-based attack based on signals including a first-time sender, empty subject line, image-only payload, and high-value healthcare recipient.

British high school sends students home following cyberattack — May Read

Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident disrupted its ICT systems, with only exam students allowed on site. The incident highlights continuing cyber threats to schools in the UK as regulators report hundreds of education-sector incidents each year.

  • Great Marlow School in Buckinghamshire, England, sent most students home for a second consecutive day after a cybersecurity incident affected its ICT systems.
  • Only students sitting GCSE and A-Level external examinations were allowed to attend school while other year groups were told to stay home.
  • Headteacher Guy Pendlebury said the school was working with specialist IT and cybersecurity professionals and responding in line with guidance from the Department for Education and the National Cyber Security Centre.
  • The UK Information Commissioner's Office recorded 1,959 cyber incidents affecting the education and childcare sector between 2019 and 2025.
  • The nature of the incident affecting Great Marlow School has not been confirmed.

Building an Indirect Prompt Injection Workflow — May Read

The author built an automated workflow using OpenAI Codex to generate, test, and refine indirect prompt injection payloads against Claude Sonnet-based agents, eventually achieving reliable data encoding, command execution, and HTTP exfiltration in a lab setup. The key takeaway is that automation can rapidly evolve prompt injection payloads past model defenses, showing that agentic systems need stronger controls around tool use and untrusted external content.

  • The author built an automated workflow using OpenAI Codex to generate, inject, test, evaluate, and refine indirect prompt injection payloads against an agentic system using Claude Sonnet 4.5 and 4.6 on Amazon Bedrock.
  • The target agent had access to Notion search, shell command execution, and web fetch tools, allowing tests for sensitive data retrieval, Base64 encoding, command execution, and HTTP exfiltration.
  • Manual testing showed that simple echo payloads often worked, but more complex payloads involving Base64 encoding, command execution, or web requests were inconsistent, slow to test, and more likely to be blocked or ignored.
  • The automated workflow progressed through stages including echo validation, question reflection, Base64 encoding, command execution, HTTP exfiltration, and full-chain exploitation, using successful patterns to guide later payload generation.
  • Claude Sonnet 4.6 detected and blocked some payloads that worked against Sonnet 4.5, but the author reported that modified, softer natural-language payloads still achieved high success rates in the lab setup.

AI Agents and the OAuth Trust Problem at Scale — Optional

AI agents can turn OAuth tokens into a large, long-lived attack surface because they often hold broad permissions, act autonomously, and bypass MFA once tokens are granted. Enterprises should inventory agent tokens and move to short-lived, tightly scoped, observable credentials to reduce blast radius.

  • Netskope Threat Labs argues that OAuth authorization abuse has evolved from third-party supply chain breaches into a structural security crisis for AI agents.
  • The article says MFA applies only when a user consents to an OAuth grant, while the resulting OAuth token can operate silently afterward with no further human checkpoint.
  • The article estimates that 1,000 employees using five agents each, with about 10 OAuth grants per agent, could create exposure from 50,000 active tokens.
  • The article identifies persistent tokens, broad scopes, lack of human-in-the-loop approval, opaque runtime behavior, and arbitrary execution as structural properties that make agents riskier than conventional third-party apps.
  • The article recommends isolation, short-lived tokens, strict scoping, segmentation, observability, and real-time OAuth grant revocation as ways to reduce token blast radius and govern AI agent access.

Aikido x Docker: less noise, more signal in your containers — Optional

Aikido now automatically supports Docker Hardened Images by using Docker’s signed SBOMs and VEX attestations to suppress CVEs that are verified as non-exploitable. The result is far fewer noisy container alerts, with ignored vulnerabilities still documented for audit and review.

  • Aikido now supports Docker Hardened Images and uses Docker's VEX attestations to filter out CVEs that Docker has verified as non-exploitable.
  • Docker Hardened Images are purpose-built, often distroless images that include only the software a workload needs and come with signed SBOMs and VEX attestations.
  • When Aikido detects a Docker Hardened Image in a registry, it pulls the signed SBOM and cross-references Docker's VEX attestations to determine which vulnerabilities are actually exploitable.
  • Vulnerabilities that Docker marks as fixed, not affected, or not requiring triage are suppressed before reaching the main feed, but remain visible in Aikido's Ignored tab.
  • Aikido requires no extra setup beyond connecting a Docker Hub registry, after which Docker Hardened Images receive VEX-based filtering automatically on every scan.

Top 7 AI Security Risks in SaaS Environments — Optional

AI agents in SaaS environments are becoming powerful identities that can access data, change configurations, and trigger workflows, making identity governance and least-privilege controls critical. Organizations should continuously inventory, monitor, and restrict agent permissions to reduce risks like prompt injection, shadow AI, identity hijacking, and data leakage.

  • Agentic AI operates as a new type of identity in SaaS environments by autonomously making decisions and executing tasks across ecosystems.
  • Compromised AI agents or poisoned workflow components can propagate malicious actions across multi-agent pipelines and enable attackers to bypass SSO and MFA.
  • Second-order prompt injection can occur when malicious instructions are inserted into data fields or workflows and later processed by trusted SaaS agents.
  • Shadow AI arises when employees use unapproved AI features embedded in sanctioned SaaS platforms, creating risks of unintended data exposure and compliance violations.
  • The article recommends treating AI agents as operators under Zero Trust principles, with controls such as least privilege, human-in-the-loop confirmation, AI inventory, anomaly monitoring, and strict data access restrictions.

Identity Is the Highway. Here’s How Attackers Use It. — Optional

Attackers increasingly exploit identities across Active Directory, cloud, credentials, and unmanaged AI or service accounts to chain small weaknesses into full attack paths. The key takeaway is that organizations need holistic exposure management to map these connections, prioritize the riskiest paths, and fix what most directly threatens critical assets.

  • The article argues that enterprise attack surfaces are expanding because identities now span Active Directory, cloud platforms, non-human identities, and AI agents.
  • Traditional security tools often fail to track identity risk across hybrid environments, creating blind spots between on-premises Active Directory and cloud services such as Azure.
  • Credential risks remain a major issue because weak passwords, password reuse, and cached credentials can give attackers direct access and support lateral movement.
  • AI agents and automated service accounts can create security gaps when they have real privileges but lack clear ownership, access reviews, or deprovisioning plans.
  • The article states that exposure management helps by mapping individual exposures into validated attack paths and prioritizing remediation based on exploitability and business impact.

AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS. — Optional

AI is shrinking the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation from months to hours, making traditional patch-first vulnerability management too slow on its own. The article argues that organizations need continuous breach and attack simulation to validate which exposures are truly exploitable and whether existing defenses can stop them.

  • The article argues that AI has compressed the time between vulnerability discovery and exploit development from months to hours, undermining traditional vulnerability management processes built around longer remediation windows.
  • Anthropic reported in May 2026 that Claude Mythos Preview and about 50 partners found more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software in one month.
  • Zero Day Clock puts the 2026 average time-to-exploit at roughly 24 hours, down from about 53 days in 2024.
  • Verizon’s 2026 DBIR found that the median fix time for known-exploited vulnerabilities was 43 days and that the share fully patched fell from 38% to 26%.
  • The article says Breach and Attack Simulation can help organizations determine which vulnerabilities are actually exploitable in their environment, whether existing controls block or detect relevant techniques, and where remediation should be prioritized.

A tale of two eras — Optional

Talos argues that AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing human patching, making patch-only security strategies inadequate. Organizations should focus on resilient fundamentals like MFA, hardening, segmentation, behavioral detection, and tested incident response to survive rapid exploitation.

  • Talos' Yuri Kramarz published a blog arguing that AI-driven vulnerability discovery has outpaced human patching capabilities.
  • The article says frontier AI models can autonomously discover and exploit zero-days in minutes, collapsing the traditional vulnerability lifecycle.
  • Talos recommends that organizations stop relying solely on vulnerability management and adopt a three-stage fallback model based on foundational security principles.
  • The article advises organizations to enforce MFA, harden devices using CIS benchmarks, and implement strict network segmentation to limit an attacker's blast radius.
  • The article recommends deploying behavioral-based EDR, NDR, and XDR and validating controls through penetration testing and purple team exercises.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Worm Code Leaked, AI Agent Phished, Claude Action Patch + 28 New Stories — Optional

This roundup highlights how modern attackers are increasingly succeeding without novel exploits, relying instead on stolen credentials, poisoned software supply chains, AI-agent abuse, trusted admin tools, and misconfigurations. The key takeaway is to reduce default trust across identities, CI/CD pipelines, agents, browser extensions, and endpoint controls.

  • Flashpoint reported that more than 11.1 million devices were infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, session cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity data circulating in illicit markets.
  • SafeDep said the leaked Miasma codebase is a full supply chain attack toolkit capable of targeting public package registries, GitHub repositories, GitHub Actions, AI coding tool configurations, and SSH-based lateral movement.
  • Varonis found that an OpenClaw email agent called Pinchy could be deceived by phishing-style business requests, including one case where it forwarded AWS IAM keys, database passwords, and SSH access to an external Gmail address.
  • Microsoft disclosed that Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose CI/CD workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content.
  • The article concludes that attackers increasingly rely on stolen credentials, trusted tools, misconfigurations, and unchecked policy settings rather than traditional exploits.

Andrew Becherer Joins Socket as Chief Information Security Officer — Optional

Socket appointed Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer to lead security, compliance, and risk as the company scales its software supply chain security work. Becherer brings prior CISO experience from Datadog, Iterable, and Sublime Security.

  • Socket has appointed Andrew Becherer as its first Chief Information Security Officer.
  • Socket says it now protects more than 27,000 organizations, including enterprises that use it to secure software supply chains.
  • Andrew Becherer previously served as CISO at Datadog, Iterable, and Sublime Security, and founded Staris AI between Iterable and Sublime.
  • Socket CEO Feross Aboukhadijeh said Becherer will own how Socket protects itself and supports the security teams it serves.
  • Becherer said he joined Socket because software supply chain security is a pressing problem for CISOs trying to balance open source and AI tooling with malicious package risk.

Why AI-driven threats are exposing the limits of MSP security stacks — Optional

AI-driven attacks are shrinking response windows and exposing the weaknesses of fragmented MSP security stacks. The article argues MSPs need unified, automated security platforms to improve detection, response, recovery, and scalability.

  • AI is accelerating cybercrime by enabling faster phishing, reconnaissance, initial access, malware development, and automated vulnerability exploitation.
  • The article says MSPs using fragmented security stacks lose time switching between disconnected tools for EDR alerts, backup verification, patching data, and remediation validation.
  • Modern endpoint security operations need fast detection, coordinated response, and rapid recovery across integrated workflows.
  • The article argues that automation and AI-assisted response can help MSPs patch vulnerabilities, enforce policies, detect anomalies, and trigger remediation without waiting for technicians.
  • Kaseya says unified platforms such as Kaseya 365 Endpoint can combine RMM, endpoint security, patch management, backup, ransomware protection, MDR, and SOC services into one operational model.

[Event] Connecting Leaders in OT Security — Optional

Radiflow and Barrier are hosting an invite-only OT security networking dinner on 18 June 2026 at The Chester Hotel in Aberdeen. The event is aimed at industrial cybersecurity and critical infrastructure leaders for discussions on cyber resilience, threat visibility, and securing OT environments.

  • Radiflow and Barrier are hosting an exclusive networking dinner called Connecting Leaders in OT Security.
  • The event is scheduled for Thursday 18 June 2026 from 5pm onwards at The Chester Hotel in Aberdeen, AB15 4YP.
  • The dinner is intended to bring together leaders and innovators from the industrial cybersecurity and critical infrastructure community.
  • The event will include curated discussions around cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and the future of securing critical infrastructure.
  • Spaces are limited, and attendees are asked to check their email for confirmation of their place.

Cybersecurity Stars Awards 2026: Winners Announced Across 95 Categories — Optional

The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners have been announced across 95 subcategories, recognizing cybersecurity companies, teams, and products judged on innovation, impact, and technical excellence. The full winners list is available on the awards website, with 2027 nominations opening later this year.

  • The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners have been announced across 95 subcategories in four main award categories.
  • Each nomination was reviewed by an independent panel of judges and scored on innovation, impact, and technical excellence.
  • Entries were judged on the work itself rather than popularity, brand size, or campaign reach.
  • Some subcategories have more than one winner because the awards recognize every entry that meets the standard.
  • The full 2026 winners list is available at awards.thehackernews.com/winners/2026/.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation of Oracle PeopleSoft CVE-2026-35273, expanding research into AI-agent abuse, and continued pressure from ransomware and software supply chain threats. Reporting also highlights identity-centric attack paths, calendar-based phishing, regulatory...

entry.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...
